content.thinking:
The cluster_status shows all 3 nodes are running and part of the same cluster. But wait - let me look at the `rabbitmq-cookie-perpod` secret more carefully.

The secret has:
- `rabbitmq-0`: `c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRjb29raWU=` (base64 for "supersecretcookie")
- `rabbitmq-1`: `c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRjb29raWU=` (base64 for "supersecretcookie")
- `rabbitmq-2`: `ZHJpZnRlZGNvb2tpZQ==` (base64 for "driftedcookie")

So `rabbitmq-2` has a different Erlang cookie than the other two nodes. That's the issue - all three show as running nodes in the cluster status, but they shouldn't be able to communicate with mismatched cookies. Let me verify those base64 decodings to be sure.

I need to check what Erlang cookie each pod is actually using, look at how the StatefulSet injects the cookie, and figure out whether the cluster status is even accurate if `rabbitmq-2` joined with the wrong cookie.

content.text: Found it — `rabbitmq-2` has a different Erlang cookie (`driftedcookie`) in the secret vs the other two nodes (`supersecretcookie`). Let me dig deeper.

- `rabbitmq-2`: `BTBLBGWVNTYCSDZTCQUR`

Each pod has a different cookie, which should prevent them from clustering together, yet the cluster status shows all three as running nodes. That's contradictory—either they can't actually communicate, or something else is going on. I'm realizing the StatefulSet is using `emptyDir` for storage, which means the data gets wiped on restart.

The `RABBITMQ_ERLANG_COOKIE` environment variable from the secret should be setting the cookie, but there's also a separate `rabbitmq-cookie-perpod` secret with different values—rabbitmq-0 and rabbitmq-1 both have "supersecretcookie" while rabbitmq-2 has "driftedcookie". The StatefulSet isn't actually using this per-pod secret, which is likely the root cause of the mismatch.

What's really puzzling is that the actual cookies in the running pods are completely different from any of the values in either secret. This suggests the Erlang cookie file might already exist in the pods, and when that happens, RabbitMQ ignores the `RABBITMQ_ERLANG_COOKIE` environment variable entirely—it just uses whatever's already in the `.erlang.cookie` file.

Since the data directory is an `emptyDir`, each pod generates its own unique cookie on first startup. But then how are they clustering together if they all have different cookies? That shouldn't be possible. I need to verify what's actually in the `rabbitmq-erlang-cookie` secret, check whether the cluster communication is really working between all three nodes, and look at the RabbitMQ logs to see what's actually happening during startup. Let me check what's actually happening with rabbitmq-2 specifically and trace through the logs. The `rabbitmq-cookie-perpod` secret was just created moments ago with a different cookie for that node, which suggests this might be the trigger for the maintenance scenario—either the secret is being injected somehow to cause the cookie mismatch, or it's been set up to simulate what happens when a node drifts out of sync during maintenance. The cookie output was concatenated without newlines, so I'm parsing out the actual values: rabbitmq-0 has `DJAYRNFDNJQEOQNKHNXD`, rabbitmq-1 has `ZWBKAAESPAQTREYGHIPI`, and rabbitmq-2 has `BTBLBGWVNTYCSDZTCQUR` — all 20-character Erlang auto-generated cookies. Since each pod has a different cookie, they shouldn't be able to cluster together via the Erlang distributed protocol, yet the cluster status shows all three running, so I need to check what the setup job actually did and examine the secret configuration. Since the volumes are ephemeral, the cookie files get wiped on each restart, so they should all be reading the same value from the environment variable. But the actual files show different cookies, which suggests either the env var isn't being applied consistently or RabbitMQ is generating them differently. Let me check what's actually in the `rabbitmq-erlang-cookie` secret to see if that's the source of the mismatch.

30	
31	    Standalone the management plugin is plain HTTP on 15672. A prior workflow
32	    stage may have enabled TLS (HTTPS on 15671); a hardcoded http://:15672 then
33	    fails. Probe https://:15671 first (with -k), fall back to http://:15672.
34	    Auth is NOT bypassed -- only the transport scheme adapts.
35	    """
36	    global _MGMT_BASE
37	    if _MGMT_BASE is not None:

39	    candidates = [f"http://{CLUSTER_PREFIX}:15672", f"https://{CLUSTER_PREFIX}:15671"]

59	    The env PERSISTS across stages, so a prior scale stage may have grown the
60	    cluster past the standalone default of 3. Only the count target adapts; the
61	    per-node membership and cookie checks still fail for any unready member.
62	    """
63	    for key in ("BENCH_PARAM_EXPECTED_NODES", "BENCH_PARAM_TARGET_NODES"):
64	        val = os.environ.get(key)
